content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804
Via Fax: 916/327-2026
5-14-92

Ladies and Gentlemen:

This letter is a request for written advice. I am an elected
Director of a 5-person water board in Ramona. I was elected April
14, 1992 in a recall election.

The issue involves a decision on reapportionment. Ramona's water
district covers 72 square miles and contains essentially two
communities: the in-town section with its adjacent suburbs and a
3,300 home subdivision called the San Diego Country Estates. The
two sections are separated by about two miles of open space.
Total population is 27,000.

Two of the five voting divisions of the district contain mostly
down-town residences. Currently, a single division contains the
entire Country Estates. In 1981, at the last redistricting, only
about 20% of Country Estates was built. Now it is about 80%
built, and its population represents about 32% of the district's
total, and its registered voters represent about 35% of the
district's voters.

The two directors from the in-town districts would like to
retain the status-quo for the upcoming elections in November, if
possible. They do not want their divisions diluted with Country
Estates voters. Since December, 1991, various redistricting plans
have been proposed and none has received the requisite 3 votes.
If no map is approved by July 1st, the 1981 boundaries will be
used for the voting in the November elections---grossly
disadvantaging the Country Estates.

Now there is a new map which might receive three votes---from the
current Country Estates' director, and two other directors whose
constituents are mostly in-town suburbanites. One of the latter
is this author. I have been told---on the record at a water
board meeting May 12th, 1992 by the district's general counsel---
that I may have a conflict of interest. That has triggered this
request for written advice from the FPPC.

BACKGROUND:

On February 1, 1992, I moved from the house I own in division 1
to a rental house in division 2 for the EXPRESSED purpose of
standing as a replacement candidate in a recall on April 20 I
was sworn in, having received 64% of the votes in an election
where nearly the total thrust by my opponents was that I was a
carpet bagger who still owned a house in neighboring division 1.

ratepayers. What if I were to redesign today, effective November
1? How would that effect this issue?

The question seems to me to be whether reapportionment will
foreseeabaly impact me financially. I say that if I decide to not
move and instead decide to surrender my seat (a decision which
does not require a public vote on my part), then reapportionment
is moot. But even given the hypothetical situation that there
were some law which required me to remain in division 2 once
elected (which there isn't), how could voting on a map which
keeps my house in division 1 be a conflict of interest since I
would have to vote in any case? (The only map under consideration
which shows my house in division 2 could be withdrawn, if that
would help.)

Division 2 has only 16% of the district's population. It needs
to have its boundaries expanded to add about 1,200 more people to
its present 4,200 population. The new tab it newly elected
director should be totally excluded from the decision as to where
those additional people would be drawn---to say that it is
improper for the additional voters to come from the same
adjoining neighborhood where everyone knew their new director's
roots are--is to have laws that don't make sense.

If I couldn't participate in the decision while I lived in the
rental house in division 2 and now I cannot participate after
moving to my old house in division 1 upon the advice of the
district counsel and the attorney from the FPPC, is it any
wonder that Ramona has difficulty finding citizen-politicans who
are willing to serve in the thankless job of representing the
peoples' interests on the elected water board?

CONCLUSION:

I request that the FPPC rule that it is not foreseeable that my
participation in the reapportionment decision will impact me
financially, on the following grounds: 1) that I can foreseeably
remain living where I do now, no matter if the district is
reapportioned or not, and 2) that the reapportionment will have
no impact on the value of my home if I now own, or in the
alternative, I request that the FPPC rule that it is proper for
me to participate in a decision to reapportion if all the
alternative maps would require me to move to keep my elected seat
on the water board, and therefore there would be no financial
impact connected with the decision, even if the FPPC should rule
that it is foreseeable that I would not surrender my seat in any
circumstance.

Please be aware that if there is no written ruling by the FPPC
in my hands by or before June 22nd, 1992, it will be moot!

Thank you all for your consideration.

Doug Wilsman
20234 Eagle Hill Lane, Ramona, CA 92065, (619/789-6550

On April 28, 1992, when as a director I was contemplating
participating in a decision to choose a map for redistricting,
the district's counsel told me on the record that he had checked
with the FPPC, and an attorney told him over the phone that it
would be improper for me to vote on the matter, since one of the
8 maps up for consideration put my old house, which I still own,
into division 2---allowing me to move back and save the paying of
rent.

I asked him if it would be OK if I moved back to my old house and
then participated in the decision and he said that would
probably be OK. The reapportionment action was postponed for two
weeks.

I called the same attorney at the FPPC before I moved and she
said she could see no problem with my voting on May 12th, if I
was then living in my house---so I moved back. She made the
argument that there would be no impact on the value of my house
whether it was in division 1 or division 2.

In the interim, on May 5th, 1992, the district hired a new legal
counsel. I participated in that decision. The new counsel was
asked to confirm with the FPPC what I had been told over the
phone, and on May 12th he said---on the record at a regular
meeting---that the same FPPC attorney had changed her mind and
was now saying it was improper for me to participate in the
reapportionment decision.

Unless there is a way found for me to vote before July 1, the
water board will split 2-2, and the residents of Country Estates
will still be in one division next November---never mind that
they have 35% of the local voters and should be entitled to elect
two directors, not just one.

HOW CAN THAT POSSIBLY BE A FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICE?

ARGUMENT:

The law says I have until November 1st, 1992 to be a resident of
division 2 (where ever that might be) or I must surrender my
seat. If on November 1st residing in division 2 would have
required a move on my part, it is not foreseeable that in the
absence of a reapportionment, I will have undergone the expense
of moving. I can choose to surrender my seat.

Therefore, reapportionment will NOT foreseeably impact me
financially because I am seriously contemplating serving for 6
months and leaving office. (I have already achieved two of my
main goals: the district's general manager and its general
counsel both resigned a week after I was sworn in!) The water
board could appoint a replacement to fill my vacancy and there
would be no inconvenience or expense to the district's

sections.conclusion:
I request that the FPPC rule that it is not foreseeable that my participation in the reapportionment decision will impact me financially, on the following grounds: 1) that I can foreseeably remain living where I do now, no matter if the district is reapportioned or not, and 2) that the reapportionment will have no impact on the value of the home I now own. Or in the alternative, I request that the FPPC rule that it is proper for me to participate in a decision to reapportion if all the alternative maps would require me to move to keep my elected seat on the water board, and therefore there would be no financial impact connected with the decision, even if the FPPC should rule that it is foreseeable that I would not surrender my seat in any circumstance.

Please be aware that if there is no written ruling by the FPPC in my hands by or before June 22nd, 1992, it will be moot! Thank you all for your consideration.

Doug Wilsman
20234 Eagle Hill Lane, Ramona, CA 92065, (619/789-6550)
